data.title: DGS: Reasonable Yield And Geographical Diversification
data.description:
Opportunity Overview

The WisdomTree Emerging Markets SmallCap Dividend Fund (NYSEARCA:DGS) looks like one of the most attractive vehicles within emerging markets at the moment. It has a long-term history of outperforming many emerging market vehicles, including dividend-focused ETFs, and it offers uncorrelated returns since it does not track traditional benchmarks like the MSCI Emerging Markets Index. As a result, it is less vulnerable to pullbacks due to tensions in key markets like China and Taiwan, as well as pullbacks in large-cap EM stocks.

This ETF has outperformed the iShares MSCI Emerging Markets ETF (EEM) by over 40 percentage points in the past five years. This ETF gained significantly while many emerging markets struggled with inflation and other issues after 2022.

This article will explore how the country/sector/company composition of this ETF makes it a standout within emerging markets and even other dividend ETFs.

ETF Overview

The Wisdom Tree Emerging Markets Small Cap Dividend Fund is an excellent alternative vehicle in emerging markets, which stands out for its high dividend yield, low valuation, and unique portfolio composition. Investing in small-cap, high dividend yielding equities can be a solid strategy during risky economic cycles. This ETF offers unique exposure to emerging markets relative to other emerging market ETFs.

Small caps: None of the companies in this ETF have a market cap above $10 billion, and half of them have a market cap below $2 billion. Many of these stocks may be off the radar of most institutional investors and, most importantly, less vulnerable to drawdowns due to broader emerging market sell-offs or index rebalancing.

Superiority of Dividend Income: Investing in stocks with a high dividend yield can be an excellent inflation hedge and means to outperform, especially when these equities are undervalued. A study since 1957 posted by Wisdom Tree found that high-yielding stocks had a greater return per annum relative to the broader S&P 500 Index.

Valuation and Yield: At the moment, this ETF trades at a 13% discount to the MSCI Emerging Markets Index on a price to earnings basis, and a 28% discount on a price to book basis. It also has a notably higher dividend yield, as the MSCI Emerging Markets Index only has a 2.7% dividend yield.

Country Allocation: This ETF only invests around 35% of its assets in China and Taiwan, compared to 47% in MSCI Emerging Markets, and it is considerably underweight China. This is a notable improvement in terms of diversification, although it is still around 7 percentage points overweight Taiwan.

Other overlooked markets like Indonesia, Turkey, Chile, and the Philippines are some of the top 15 countries in this ETF, although they each account for less than 3% of this ETF.

This ETF also has a very unique country allocation relative to the MSCI Emerging Markets Small Cap Index.

Below are some of my thoughts on this portfolio composition:

  • Taiwan: Taiwan will still face strong tariffs from the US economy, and was very vulnerable in 2023 due to the volatility of the semiconductor industry. The equity markets in Taiwan are up by around 12% this year, and could be vulnerable to pullbacks amid any new trade war tensions. Taiwan still looks like a good hold at the moment, especially since key companies may be exempt from Trump's 100% tariffs on semiconductors.
  • China: Chinese equities have also rallied this year despite trade war tensions, outperforming many dominant markets like Taiwan, South Korea, and other larger Asian markets. This ETF is slightly underweight China relative to the MSCI Emerging Markets Small Cap Index, and also around 18 percentage points underweight relative to the MSCI Emerging Markets Index. Being underweight China seems ideal now, especially following its H1 2025 rally.
  • India: Being underweight India may also lead to underperformance in the short term. Equities in this market already trade at 27x earnings and 3.8x book value, a hefty premium to other Asian emerging markets. India has also been one of the main targets of US tariffs due to its purchasing of Russian oil.
  • Other Overweight Decisions: This ETF is also significantly overweight undervalued markets like Brazil and Malaysia, which will lead to uncorrelated returns relative to other indexes.

Unique Industry Approach: One of the main drivers of emerging market performance has been from some of the dominant tech names in the market. This ETF takes a different approach by ignoring the noise and focusing on quality, high-yielding small caps in emerging markets.

Sectors like information technology and financials, which dominate many EM funds, take more of a back seat approach with this ETF, accounting for circa 31% of its assets. This allows for more exposure to consumer, real estate, and healthcare small caps, which are ignored by many larger funds that track the MSCI Emerging Markets Index.

Of course, there is a price to pay in the short term when emerging equities enter a bull market. Dominant emerging market names like Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) and HDFC Bank (HDB), several top 5 constituents of many emerging market funds, have outperformed this ETF.

However, this Wisdom Tree ETF has outperformed its emerging market benchmark peer, as well as many of the other dominant EM stocks, which have had massive drawdowns in the past five years. EM growth investing is all about timing and choosing the best names, while this Wisdom Tree ETF is a more surefire bet, which has still delivered a stellar 64% return in the past five years.

EM Performance Varies

There has been a massive divergence in the performance of emerging markets this year, with a notable surprise from many Asian markets that have defied trade war tensions, as they did during Trump's previous administration.

China and Taiwan have been two of the key drivers of positive performance in emerging Asian markets, which has largely explained the bull run in emerging market ETFs this year.

While this ETF has been a relative underperformer this year, it may be a safer bet in H2 2025 if larger emerging equity markets experience pullbacks, especially since many of these ETFs are heavily concentrated in large caps, with over half of their assets invested in a small handful of markets.

A Unique Vehicle Poised to Outperform Benchmark

A comparison of the long-term performance of this ETF to other dividend ETFs shows two trends:

1) This ETF has been a superior long-term vehicle relative to other emerging market dividend funds, outperforming many other dividend funds. This ETF, and even other Wisdom Tree ETFs, look like a clear victor if you are focused on emerging market dividend investing.

2) Standard dividend funds, which primarily focus on US equities, have trounced this ETF in the past decade.

The top-performing ETF, the Vanguard Dividend Appreciation ETF (VYM), has a much lower yield at the moment and primarily focuses on major large caps in the United States.

When comparing this ETF to U.S.-focused ETFs, the picture is a bit more murky. These Vanguard vehicles have been driven more by capital gains, and currently offer a much lower dividend yield.

The S&P 500 has also trounced these companies in terms of capital gains. In essence, these ETFs have functioned more like safer, growth ETFs.

If one is focused on emerging markets, this WisdomTree Emerging Market Smallcap Dividend Fund looks like one of the safer bets in the market, as it has historically outperformed and may be less vulnerable to drawdowns. This ETF has had a solid run in the past couple of years, resulting in a slightly lower yield at the moment. However, it may not be the best international dividend ETF. The iShares International Dividend ETF (IDV), which has also performed in line with this ETF, is an attractive vehicle for dividend investors that offers a slightly higher yield at the moment.

data.title: Bull-Bear Spread Tips Negative
data.description:
The equity market's rally has hit a bit of a snag since late July, although the S&P 500 is far from having collapsed as it remains within a couple percentage points of record highs. Nonetheless, sentiment has taken a hit. Bullish sentiment according to the weekly AAII survey peaked in the first week of July at 45%. Since then, it has fallen in four of the five weeks, with the latest print of 34.9% the lowest of that stretch. That is only the lowest reading since the week of June 18th, when it fell to 33.2%.

The drop in bulls corresponds with bearish sentiment picking up. Bearish sentiment has seen a more substantial increase, rising from 33% last week to 43.2% this week. That is now the highest reading for bears since the week of May 15, and the 10.2 percentage point leap week over week was the largest increase since the last week of February.

As shown below, the spread between bulls and bears has dipped back into negative territory and is at its lowest level since mid-May.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Commodities: Potential Trump-Putin Meeting Weighs On Oil
data.description:
By Warren Patterson, Ewa Manthey

Energy - Potential Trump-Putin meeting

ICE Brent settled almost 0.7% lower yesterday with hopes that Presidents Trump and Putin may meet soon, possibly as soon as next week. It’s still unclear if Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy will take part. This is important because it could affect the secondary tariffs on India, depending on how discussions go. However, it is important to note that President Trump’s deadline for a Russia-Ukraine peace deal expires today, leaving open the risk that the US will still tighten sanctions against Moscow.

Meanwhile, there are media reports that Indian state refiners are taking a step back from buying Russian crude oil amid the tariff uncertainty. Refiners are waiting for some guidance from the government before resuming their purchases. As we have mentioned previously, Indian exports to the US dwarf the savings that India receives from buying discounted Russian crude oil. Therefore, we believe that India will likely switch to alternative crude supply in order to avoid these additional tariffs. This should lead to increased demand for other grades from the Middle East, continuing to provide support to Dubai relative to Brent.

The latest trade data from China shows that crude oil imports in July averaged 11.2m b/d, up 11.5% year on year but down a little more than 8% month on month. Imports in June were strong as independent refiners restocked. This leaves cumulative imports so far this year at 11.3m b/d over the first seven months of the year, up 3.2% YoY.

Metals - China buys more gold

China’s central bank continues to add gold to its reserves. In July, the People’s Bank of China increased its gold reserves for the ninth straight month. Gold held by the central bank increased by 60,000 troy ounces to 73.96 million troy ounces last month. Gold is one of the strongest-performing major commodities, up by more than a quarter this year amid Trump’s aggressive trade policy, conflicts in the Middle East and Ukraine, and central bank buying.

Preliminary China’s Customs data showed strong domestic demand for industrial metals. Imports of unwrought copper rose 9.1% YoY (+4.3% MoM) to 480kt in July. However, cumulative copper imports are still down 2.7% YoY to 3.1mt in the first seven months of the year. Uncertainty over US tariffs on copper imports shifted supply from China to the US in the first half of the year. This trend may reverse in the second half, as Trump drops plans for a 50% tariff on refined copper. Meanwhile, copper concentrate imports increased 18% YoY (+8.9% MoM) to 2.6mt last month, as strong domestic refined output boosted demand for raw materials. On a year-to-date basis, imports of copper concentrate rose 7.7% YoY to 17.3mt.

In ferrous metals, iron ore imports rose 1.8% YoY to 104.6mt in July. However, cumulative iron ore imports are still down 2.4% YoY to 697.2mt in the first seven months of the year.

On the export side, China’s shipments of unwrought aluminium and aluminium products fell by 8.1% YoY to 542.1kt, while exports of steel products surged 25.6% YoY to 9.8mt last month. This is despite mounting trade barriers from countries concerned about a surge in Chinese shipments.

Agriculture - Coffee rises on lower Brazilian exports

Arabica coffee futures extended their rally, with prices settling 1.5% higher yesterday. This followed falling shipments from top producer Brazil. Brazilian Trade Ministry data shows that green coffee exports in July fell 20% YoY, largely due to farmers holding back supplies in anticipation of higher prices amid uncertainty over potential US tariffs on Brazilian coffee. Looking at inventories, official data shows that total coffee stocks at ICE monitored warehouses declined for a ninth consecutive day to 751.04k bags, the lowest since May 2024.

China Customs data shows that China’s soybean imports rose 18.5% YoY to 11.7mt in July, although down 4.8% MoM. The increase in imports was largely driven by strong Brazilian flows. This leaves cumulative soybean imports at 61.04mt, up 4.6% YoY.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Airbnb: Elevated Valuation Meets A Weakening Economy
data.description:
No matter how wonderful [a business] is, it's not worth an infinite price. We have to have a price that makes sense and gives a margin of safety.

- Charlie Munger

We began covering Airbnb (NASDAQ:ABNB)(NEOE:ABNB:CA) after the huge IPO excitement had sent shares to what we viewed as excessive levels, which is why our rating opinion was a "Sell". Since then the company has underperformed the S&P 500 Index by more than 50%. More recently, we saw shares reasonably valued and changed our view to a "Buy" rating. Since then, the stock is up almost 50%, though they are still slightly underperforming the S&P 500 Index.

Now with the economy showing clear signs of slowing down, and travel being one of the most discretionary categories, we think it is a good time to reevaluate the current valuation and recent financial performance, especially after Airbnb just reported results for Q2.

Cautious consumer sentiment was reflected in Airbnb's North America performance in the second quarter results, the company's most strategic market, where it delivered low single-digit year-over-year growth. Europe was not that much better, with the company generating mid-single-digits year-over-year growth. Smaller markets like Latin America and Japan continue to see healthy growth, but due to weakness in its core markets Airbnb only saw a 7% year-over-year overall increase in nights and seats booked. It seems things did not improve after the quarter ended, as management said they were "seeing quite depressed bookings in July".

At the end of the day, Airbnb is a travel company, and travel remains a highly discretionary spending item. Recessions and growth slow-downs provide a good reminder of this fact, with the Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLY) often underperforming the market during times of economic uncertainty. For its part, the Amplify Travel Tech ETF (AWAY) has lost investors money since its launch a few years ago.

Second Quarter Financial Results

Airbnb delivered a solid second quarter, generating $3.1 billion in revenue, a 13% year-over-year increase, and net income of $642 million, representing a 21% net income margin. Gross Booking Value (GBV) of $23.5 billion represented organic growth of 9% year-over-year, excluding foreign exchange effects. Nights and seats booked grew more modestly, at only 7% year-over-year as previously mentioned.

Weakening Consumer

Weakness in the U.S. consumer is spreading to more segments of the population, as can be seen in a surge in past-due student debt, credit card, and auto loan delinquencies even for top earners. Consumers themselves have been expressing increased frustration with economic conditions, with consumer sentiment at very low levels.

Another sign that travel is likely cooling off is that growth in total vehicle miles traveled appears to be stalling, despite very low gasoline prices. At the same time international tourism to the U.S. is facing declines, which means Airbnb might have a hard time even maintaining single-digit growth in its most important market.

The fact that vacations have become significantly more expensive, outpacing general inflation over the past decade, make it increasingly difficult for consumers to splurge on vacations, going out to restaurants, or some of the new experiences Airbnb is promoting.

Financials

Airbnb's growth has decelerated more than we would have expected, with the recent 9% quarterly year-over-year growth rate in organic GBV making it hard to justify its stretched valuation multiples. Profits increased compared to Q2 of FY2024, but actually decreased compared to Q2 of FY2023 due to lower profit margins.

The adjusted numbers are more flattering, but this is in large part because of the heavy use of stock-based compensation by Airbnb. It is not only quite high, but has also been increasing in recent quarters.

To be fair, the company has reduced shares outstanding in recent years thanks to its significant buybacks, but the reduction would have been more significant if stock-based compensation was more measured.

Regulatory Headwinds

It is well known that many cities in the U.S. and around the world put some of the blame of the housing affordability crisis on short-term rental platforms like Airbnb. This has resulted in increased regulatory pressure, as evidenced by Spain's order to Airbnb to take tens of thousands of listings down.

This will likely have a significant impact on Airbnb, as Spain is the world's second-most important tourist destination, only behind France. If measured by tourism spend, it is actually the first, as visitors in France spent €71 billion last year, compared with €126 billion in Spain.

New Growth Initiatives

During the most recent earnings call, CEO Brian Chesky mentioned their "next chapter" strategy as an area that should help drive long-term growth. They teased this revamp, saying that "Now you can Airbnb more than an Airbnb," and went on to describe their new Airbnb Services and Airbnb Experiences strategy. During the second quarter earnings call Chesky boasted about the media exposure the launch created.

Now this was our biggest launch to date, and it generated more than 13,000 press stories and nearly 660 million social media impressions.

However, he sounded less confident when asked by an analyst what type of attach rate they are seeing for these services and experiences. During the prepared remarks the company also said that they do not expect meaningful revenue from their new businesses in the near term, despite $200 million of investment towards new businesses in 2025.

There's a few things. I mean, attach rate, we're absolutely looking at. We don't have any numbers to share as far as what we see for potential attach rate.

These range from personal trainers, to manicure and pedicure, to even some bizarre examples such as fighting a "Luchador," which apparently is a real experience they are offering, though we would be very surprised if these types of niche offerings move the needle for the company. Airbnb is also planning to increase marketing expenditures to promote these offerings, which is likely to further pressure profit margins.

The company is also competing with established platforms such as OpenTable for restaurant reservations in the U.S., which is owned by competitor Booking Holdings (BKNG), and with experience platform Viator owned by Tripadvisor (TRIP), as well as with TheFork for restaurant reservations in Europe, which is also owned by Tripadvisor.

Future Outlook

For the third quarter the company is guiding revenue in the range of $4.02 billion to $4.1 billion, representing year-over-year growth of 8% to 10%. As for nights and seats booked, expectations are for similar growth to what was delivered in the second quarter. Airbnb expects a decline in profit margins due to growth investments, including the approximately $200 million of investment towards new businesses in 2025, and they don't expect meaningful revenue from these new businesses in the near term.

Brian Chesky is clearly not satisfied with their current growth rate of roughly 10%, the problem is that economic conditions are likely to make this difficult, particularly in its key markets of North America and Europe. Experiences and services so far have not really shown much traction, other than getting some media attention.

We are not satisfied with the company growing approximately 10% year-over-year. We want the company to reaccelerate. We think we have a great plan to reaccelerate.

During the call an analyst hinted that Airbnb was underperforming Booking Holdings in Europe, which is concerning, as it is a key market, "And the same question on EMEA. I think you're growing slower than even your more scaled competitor in EMEA at this point."

Valuation

Airbnb shares appear to be pricing strong growth, with little room for error should consumer weakness persist, or get worse. A Price/Earnings ratio, both trailing-twelve-months and based on forward estimates, is steep for a company that is delivering revenue growth of around 10%, and that is facing increasing headwinds. At the same time we do believe the company has strong competitive advantages, for example about 90% of their website traffic comes from direct and unpaid sources.

As a result, we are therefore adjusting our rating opinion to "Hold," as we continue to believe the company has a competitive moat from network effects, but the valuation is high and risks have increased given the macroeconomic environment.

Risks

In addition to the well-known risks that Airbnb faces, such as economic conditions, regulation, and foreign exchange, we see some new ones with the increased focus on services and experiences. It adds operational risks, and this will require investments with uncertain returns. There is also a risk of a trading down to less expensive listings, or reducing trip length, although management said during the earnings call that they are not seeing yet evidence of this trend.

Finally, we see a risk with the company increasingly relying on AI customer service, as this is a critical touch point and could result in dissatisfaction or loss of trust in the brand. During the earnings call the company boasted that its AI customer service agent in the U.S. reduced the percentage of hosting guests who need to contact a human agent by 15%, but there was no mention as to customer satisfaction changes.

Conclusion

Airbnb is not invulnerable to weakness in the travel sector, or to macroeconomic headwinds. Shares are trading at elevated valuation multiples, while growth has simultaneously decelerated.

The expansion to new catering services and experiences has some promise, but comes with added costs and risks. The company is facing increased headwinds in its key markets of North America and Europe, with more complicated economic and regulatory environments. We are less optimistic about the company, especially in the short to medium term, though we continue to believe the company has a strong competitive moat.

data.title: ISM Services PMI Barely Hangs On To Expansion
data.description:
The American services engine came more than one point under expectations but stayed in the green by the slimmest of margins last month, even as price pressures roared back to life and trade frictions deepened. The Institute for Supply Management’s Services Purchasing Managers’ Index (PMI) came in at 50.1 for July, just a whisker above the 50-point line that separates growth from contraction. While that technically marks a second straight month of expansion, the reading slipped 0.7 percentage point from June and now sits more than two points below the 12-month average. Markets greeted the tepid data nervously - especially after record-setting $3,383 per-ounce print for gold, a classic barometer of distrust in fiat stability.

Digging beneath the headline, the details skew soft. Business Activity cooled to 52.6, New Orders eased to 50.3, and the Employment Index shrank to 46.4 - its fourth contraction in five months. Manufacturing looks even shakier: July’s factory PMI dropped to 48.0, extending a year-long stretch of sub-50 readings. Survey respondents blamed “higher tariffs” for escalating input costs and for shelving capital projects - a reminder that when politicians weaponize trade, real production often gets caught in the crossfire.

Source: ismworld.org

Supply lines are showing new strains as well. Supplier Deliveries rose to 51.0 for an eighth consecutive month, signaling slower freight times amid mounting port and rail congestion. Inventories remain in modest expansion, yet the Backlog of Orders has now contracted for five straight months, hinting that demand could be faltering faster than warehouses can adjust. Internationally, both export and import gauges flipped from growth to contraction in July, underscoring how tit-for-tat levies are chilling cross-border commerce.

None of that compares to the heat in the Prices Index, which jumped to 69.9 - its hottest print since October 2022 and the eighth straight month above 60. Tariffs get part of the blame, but persistent monetary largesse also plays a role; when new dollars chase fewer goods, stickers rise. It’s no coincidence that gold, the ultimate store of value for thousands of years, now changes hands above $3,300 an ounce while mainstream pundits keep insisting inflation is “cooling.” Investors appear unconvinced.

While eleven service industries - including Transportation & Warehousing and Wholesale Trade - managed growth in July, seven sectors such as Accommodation & Food Services and Construction slid into contraction. With labor demand weakening, costs climbing, and new trade barriers hitting U.S. shores, the razor-thin 50.1 PMI offers little comfort.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Carlyle Group: Strong Fundamentals Reflected In Valuation (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Shares of the Carlyle Group (NASDAQ:CG) have been an excellent performer over the past year, gaining 58%. Strong asset flows and optimism over the regulatory environment have helped to push shares near a 52-week high. The company reported solid Q2 results on Wednesday, sending CG up another 3+%. I last covered shares in May, rating CG a “buy,” and since then, the stock is up 35%, far exceeding even my bullish expectations. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit CG.

In the company’s second quarter, Carlyle earned $0.91 of distributable EPS, beating consensus by $0.02. This was another solid quarter as the company continues to see strong inflows and growing AUM. Indeed, AUM grew by 7% from last year to $465 billion, and fee-earning assets grew by 6% to $325 billion. As you can see below, the company is seeing growth across all of its platforms, though private equity is growing more slowly. While most funds are locked up for several years, CG also has $101 billion of perpetual capital, providing a recurring source of revenue. Thanks to growing AUM, fee-related earnings rose by $50 million from last year to $323 million.

I would also note that about 75% of its distributable earnings come from management fees and the rest from realized performance. While performance fees get so much of the focus, most of the company’s cash flows come from the much more predictable management fees that the platform generates. This is one reason I do not see the business as being as cyclical as often perceived. Of course, performance fees are a nice benefit, and there was $88 million of realized performance revenue. 84% of performance fee funds are in-carry, up from 76% a year ago, which supports further growth in performance revenue. Overall, fund management fees increased to $590 million from $526 million last year.

Carlyle’s fundraising shows no signs of slowing down. During the quarter, there were $13.4 billion of inflows. Global credit continues to see the lion’s share of demand with $5.5 billion of flows. CG also deployed $14.6 billion. Still, it has $89 billion of dry powder, and deploying this capital will help to further boost fee revenue—it acts similar to a backlog for an industrial firm, providing highly visible future revenue growth. Plus, Carlyle’s funding success continues with $9 billion in commitments for a tenth real estate fund, with an increased focus on residential properties.

I would also note that the Trump Administration is considering regulatory changes that would make it easier to sell private assets into 401(K) plans, potentially providing another sales channel for its private products. Moreover, that incremental demand could lift valuations and support enhanced performance fees in the medium-term. Even with a change, plan sponsors would need to amend policies and approve funds, so this is likely to be a source of moderate flows that takes time to ramp, rather than a sudden rush.

Looking at segment results, while private equity is CG’s second biggest segment by AUM, it is the most important for earnings because management fees are higher here. It generated $232 million of distributable earnings, up 16% from last year. Fund management fees were basically flat at $309 million, but net performance fees jumped to $84 million from $48 million. It still has $42 billion of available capital. The majority of funds are over 4 years old—prime time for earning performance fees, though I expect some slowing from exceptional Q2 levels.

Global Credit distributable earnings rose by 21% to $121 million as management fees increased by $46 million to $240 million while operating expenses rose by just $16 million. This unit has just $2 million in net performance revenue. Growth was flattered by $1 million of catch-up payments, and I see underlying growth as being closer to 10% here. It deployed $9 billion during the quarter. Given demand from insurance companies for private assets, I see this unit having the most growth potential.

Its AlpInvest platform saw tremendous growth with earnings of $78 million from $44 million a year ago. This is due to higher management fees with net performance revenue of just $2 million. There is meaningful demand for secondaries. This is an area I see enjoying the most benefit in the near-term if private investments are allowed in 401(K)s as we see existing private investors sell off a piece of their portfolio to retail-focused private funds.

I would also note that Carlyle has $2.9 billion of accrued performance revenue. Even if markets just hold steady at this level, this represents future revenue for the company as it exits investments. The vast majority of this sits inside of private equity of course. Similar to how its dry powder provides certainty of future fee revenue growth, this accrued performance provides visibility into future performance revenue.

Carlyle continues to have an excellent balance sheet with $1.3 billion of cash and $1.9 billion of debt. I view its 2.3% dividend yield as highly secure. During Q2, it also did $104 million of repurchases, though these primarily just offset the dilution of stock-based compensation. Aside from its cash, Carlyle also has $3 billion invested in its strategies.

I see about $13/share of value from CG’s investments and accrued investment performance. At current market levels, CG also has about $3.40-$3.55 in fee-related earnings power, which I continue to believe should be valued at ~15x, given their recurring nature. That points to $52 of value, or $65 including its investments. Back in May, I was targeting $55, given a 15x multiple on its fee-related earnings and $12.50 for its net investment and accrued performance. However, the substantial increase in the market and ongoing inflows continue to boost run-rate fees.

At $65, shares have just 3% upside. Now, given potential deregulation, it is possible flows could accelerate more, leading to multiple expansions. However, it feels like much of this enthusiasm is now in valuation. With modest upside and regulatory tailwinds, I remain a “hold” on Carlyle and see no rush to sell shares. However, there is not enough upside after such a strong run to reiterate a “buy.” Most of the outperformance has occurred, and future performance is more likely to be in line with the market.

data.title: Cognex: Caution Warranted Given Macro Uncertainty
data.description:
Cognex (NASDAQ:CGNX) reported solid results in Q2, helping to allay fears around the potential impact of tariffs. I believe caution is now warranted though, as the demand environment remains soft, and the impact of tariffs is only just starting to work its way through the economy.

I previously suggested that Cognex was attractively priced, despite near-term headwinds, and the stock is now up over 30% since then. While increased automation, enabled by AI, should ensure the business performs well longer term, economic weakness is likely to become more apparent in coming quarters. I am also now less optimistic regarding potential reshoring tailwinds, given how the tariff situation has played out so far. Cognex remains a high-quality company, but it is now more fully priced based on the uncertain macro environment.

Market Conditions

While there remains concerns about economic conditions and the potential impact of tariffs, the demand environment appears to have been fairly stable in the second quarter. Conditions remain variable across end markets though.

  • Cognex's logistics revenue continues to grow at a double-digit pace. While strength is broad-based, growth is being driven by large e-commerce players.
  • Automotive revenue continues to decline, albeit at a more modest pace than in 2024. Cognex remains cautious about the outlook for its automotive business.
  • Cognex's packaging revenue was up by a mid single digit percentage in Q2, driven by healthcare and FMCG and the company's sales force transformation. The healthcare part of the packaging business is being supported by a number of blockbuster drugs in areas like obesity and oncology.
  • Cognex's semi business decelerated in the second quarter, likely due to the uncertainty caused by tariffs.
  • Cognex's Consumer Electronics outlook for 2025 has strengthened in recent months, which could be due to the shift of manufacturing from China to other Asian countries. Cognex is also trying to capture more value by offering more complete solutions, which has been enabled by its acquisition of Moritex.

The number of Cognex job openings also points towards a fairly stable demand environment, although a recent decline could be suggestive of weakness. The primary concern at this point should probably be whether a combination of tariffs and pressure on consumer spending leads to a decline in logistics and packaging revenue growth.

Cognex Business Updates

Cognex offers AI enabled solutions for industrial machine vision applications, positioning the company to capitalize on rising adoption of automation and rapidly improving AI capabilities. Cognex is currently focused on improving the customer experience, supported by a unified product ecosystem. The company is also trying to double its customer base, largely by serving smaller manufacturers.

Cognex's OneVision cloud-based platform is an important part of its effort to reach a larger customer base. OneVision helps manufacturers build and deploy AI-enabled vision tools, and feedback from early adopters has been positive. Regulation is also driving demand in packaging, as vision solutions help with traceability. In-Sight 8900 is a newly launched AI-enabled vision system targeting this area.

Financial Analysis

Cognex generated 249 million USD in the second quarter, an increase of 4% YoY. Broader factory automation was stronger in Q2, driven by consumer electronics and packaging. In terms of geographies, the Americas and other Asia were growth drivers in Q2. Greater China revenue declined modestly due to shifts in consumer electronics supply chains, while Europe revenue was up slightly, which is a positive given current automotive headwinds.

Third quarter revenue is expected to be between 245 and 265 million USD, representing a 9% YoY increase at the midpoint, driven by logistics and factory automation. This figure excludes one off revenue from a partnership Cognex recently entered into. The partnership is targeting medical lab automation and is expected to provide an 8-14 million USD revenue boost in Q3. More typical seasonality is expected to return in Q4, resulting in a high single digit sequential revenue decline. This likely means that fourth quarter YoY growth will be in the low single digits, even though Cognex is benefitting from a slight FX tailwind.

Cognex's gross margins remain under pressure, due to a combination of tariffs and revenue mix. In particular, Moritex and Cognex's logistics business have been growth drivers recently and have lower margins. Cognex's emerging customer initiative should be a gross margin tailwind longer term though.

Cognex's adjusted operating expenses declined by 2% YoY in the second quarter. As a result, the company's operating margin continued to improve, with Cognex's adjusted EBITDA margin reaching almost 21%. Trailing twelve month free cash flow was also 180 million USD, at roughly a 20% margin. Returning capital to shareholders remains a focus area for Cognex, with the company's average diluted share count declining by 2% YoY as a result.

Conclusion

I believe Cognex's current revenue multiple is reasonable given the strength of the company and its likely margins once it has moved past the current period of investment. Cognex expects around a 10% market CAGR through the cycle, with potential for market share gains, largely through M&A. As a result, Cognex could still generate low teen returns from current levels. The stock is now far less attractive than it was a few months ago though.

Cognex's share price could easily drop below 25 USD again if the macro environment continues to deteriorate. I expect tariffs to negatively impact Cognex's logistics business at some point in the next 12 months, and the company's automotive and consumer electronics exposure are also likely to remain headwinds.

data.title: Somnigroup International Inc. 2025 Q2 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Somnigroup International Inc. in conjunction with their 2025 Q2 earnings call.
